title: Chapter 20: The Dress
content: The château was quiet in a way Jane had never heard it.

Edward had left at dawn — Paris, board prep for the IPO, a day of power points and projector screens and men in navy suits who spoke in terms of valuation multiples and market positioning. He'd kissed her forehead at the door, one hand on the back of her neck in that way he had, possessive and absent at once. "I'll be back by dinner," he'd said. "Don't burn the place down."
And then the Mercedes had swept down the gravel drive, and the iron gates had closed, and Jane had stood in the entrance hall and listened.
Nothing.

No atelier machines. No Adèle practicing scales. No Grace Poole's heavy tread on the landing above. Just the Loire wind shouldering against the old stone, and somewhere deep in the house, a pipe ticking as it cooled.
She was alone at Thornfield for the first time.

The feeling was strange — not frightening, not liberating, just unfamiliar. Like taking off a piece of jewelry you've worn so long you've forgotten the weight of it, and then your hand feels oddly light. She wandered through the rooms for a while, touching the back of a chair, the edge of a console table, each surface still holding the morning chill. The house felt different without Edward in it. Smaller. Less calculated. The walls were just walls. The art was just art. The silence was just silence, not a weapon.
By midafternoon, she found herself standing in her suite, looking at the vintage suitcase.

It sat in the far corner of the dressing room, behind the door, where the housekeeping staff had placed it on her first night and where it had remained ever since — a brown leather thing, scarred and soft, with brass clasps that had gone green at the edges. She'd unpacked the necessities in those first frantic days: toiletries, underthings, the sensible neutrals she'd thought appropriate for a French château. But the rest of it — the life she'd carried from London — she'd left packed. There hadn't been time. There hadn't been space. The Thornfield wardrobe had arrived like a decree, all cream and charcoal and dove grey, and her own clothes had seemed suddenly irrelevant, like documents written in a language this house didn't speak.
She knelt and unclasped the lid.

The smell hit her first. Old leather and cold coffee and something underneath that was simply, unmistakably, herself. Her London flat — the one above the charity shop on Marchmont Street with the radiator that clanked and the window that didn't seal and the sound of buses grinding up the hill at midnight. The life she'd had before Thornfield, before Edward, before the engagement ring and the atelier and all of it.

The clothes tumbled out as if eager. A 1990s Yohji Yamamoto jacket, black, with sleeves that swallowed her hands — she'd found it in a market stall in Brick Lane for twelve pounds and worn it to every presentation she'd ever given. Band tees: Sleater-Kinney, Björk, Fleetwood Mac rumoured tour dates from 2009. The vintage Levi's with the repaired knee, the white shirt with the ink stain on the cuff she'd never been able to shift. Each piece was worn soft at the edges, shaped by her body, carrying the memory of every room she'd entered wearing it.
She held the Yohji jacket to her face and breathed in.
London. Herself. Before.
Then, at the bottom of the suitcase, folded with a care that seemed almost ceremonial: the crimson dress.

1970s raw silk. Full skirt, nipped waist, boat neck that would show her collarbones. She'd bought it from a dealer in Kensington four years ago — an estate piece, the dealer had said, from a woman who'd worn it to the opera once and then never again, as if the dress had been too much even for her. Jane had tried it on in the shop's cramped fitting room and stared at herself in the mirror and thought: yes. This. Whatever this is. She'd been saving it ever since. For what, she didn't know. For the right occasion. For the moment when she needed to be the most herself she could possibly be.

She stood and held it up now, the fabric falling in a cascade of color that seemed almost to generate its own light. The crimson was vital, arterial — a red that didn't ask permission. Against the oatmeal walls of the suite, it was obscene. It was a shout in a library. It was a fire in a field of wheat.
It didn't belong here.
Neither did she.
That was the point.

She laid it across the bed, smoothing the skirt, adjusting the bodice so it lay as it would on a body — her body. The raw silk was rough under her fingertips, alive with texture, nothing like the smooth, sterile finishes of the Thornfield pieces. It had weight and grain and imperfection. It was real in a way that the clothes Edward had chosen for her were not.
She was still standing there, looking at it, when she heard the footsteps in the corridor.

Edward stood in the doorway of the suite, his coat still on, his keys still in hand. He must have come straight from the car. His eyes moved from Jane's face to the dress on the bed, and his expression shifted — not quickly, not obviously, but Jane had learned to read him in the micro-movements, the flickers behind the mask. Surprise first: he hadn't expected this. Then calculation: what did this mean, what was she doing, how did it change things. And then something else, something that lived beneath both — a tightening around the eyes that she'd come to recognize as fear.
"You're not going to wear that, are you?"
The question was casual. Light. The same tone he might use to ask if she was taking an umbrella. The subtext was not.

She watched him. He was leaning against the doorframe, arms crossed, the posture of a man observing a minor inconvenience — a wine stain, a scheduling conflict, a fiancée who'd briefly misplaced her sense of occasion. But his jaw was set, just slightly. His fingers pressed into the wool of his coat sleeve.
"It's beautiful," she said neutrally.
"It is." He said it the way you might say it about a dangerous animal — acknowledging the beauty while calculating the threat. "But it doesn't suit the Thornfield aesthetic."

There it was. Translation: It doesn't suit the Jane I'm building. The Jane who fits here. The Jane who is becoming part of this house — its colors, its lines, its careful, curated restraint. The crimson dress was too loud. Too uncontrolled. Too absolutely, defiantly her.

She could have argued. She could have explained — the history of the piece, the craftsmanship, the way raw silk from the 1970s had a hand that modern fabrics couldn't replicate. She could have pointed out that the Thornfield aesthetic, whatever it was, had been constructed by a man who wanted his women to disappear into the architecture. She could have said any of it.
She didn't.
Instead, she looked at the dress for a long moment. Then she looked at Edward. Then she picked it up — gently, carefully, as if it were something precious — and walked to the closet.
"You're right," she said. "Not yet."

She hung the dress at the back, behind the Thornfield pieces — the cream blazer, the charcoal dress, the dove-grey silk. Hidden but not gone. Suppressed but not destroyed. She closed the closet door and turned back to Edward.

His face had relaxed. The tension in his jaw released. The smile came — warm, relieved, the smile of a man who believed his world had been restored to its proper order. He crossed the room and kissed her, his hand on the back of her neck again.
"My girl," he murmured. "You understand."
She understood far more than he wanted her to.
That night, after dinner — a quiet one, Edward distracted by emails from the board, Jane picking at her food and thinking about a crimson dress folded behind a wall of grey — she went downstairs.

The basement corridor was cool against her bare arms, the air carrying that distinctive chill of rooms that had been carved into stone centuries ago and had never quite warmed since. She could hear the machine before she saw it — Bertha's sewing machine, running in its odd, syncopated rhythm, a stitch pattern that was entirely her own.

Bertha looked up when Jane entered. The workroom was the same as always: bolts of fabric stacked against the walls, the cutting table covered in pattern pieces, the air thick with the smell of sizing and thread and something else — something sharp and chemical that Jane had begun to associate with Bertha's late-night work, the private projects she kept under muslin when Grace Poole made her rounds.
"You're late," Bertha said. But her voice held no complaint. Just observation.
"Had to wait until he was asleep." Jane pulled a stool up to the work table. "He came back early from Paris."
"Trouble?"
"No." Jane paused. She'd been turning the moment over in her mind all evening — Edward in the doorway, the dress on the bed, the quick flash of fear behind his eyes. "I found my red dress today."
Bertha's hands stilled on the fabric. Her dark eyes sharpened. "The one from London?"
"The one from London." Jane nodded. "I took it out. I laid it on the bed. He walked in and saw it."
The silence stretched between them, loaded.
"What did he say?"
"He said it didn't suit the Thornfield aesthetic."
Bertha made a sound — not quite a laugh, not quite a scoff. Something that lived in the space between fury and recognition.
"And what did you say?"
"I said, 'You're right. Not yet.' And I hung it in the back of the closet."

Bertha stared at her. Then, slowly, her face split into a grin — wide and real and unguarded, the first full grin Jane had ever seen on her. It transformed her entirely. The sharp angles softened. The guarded eyes sparked with something that looked almost like delight.
"You kept it?" Bertha said.
"I hid it," Jane corrected. "Difference."
Bertha's grin didn't waver. "Same thing, in this house."
She set down the fabric she'd been working on and reached under the cutting table, pulling out a garment bag — plain black, unmarked, zipped tight.
"Come here," Bertha said. "I want to show you something."
She unzipped the bag.

The jacket inside was unlike anything Jane had seen at Thornfield. A tuxedo jacket, deconstructed — the seams exposed, the lining pulled partially to the outside, the shoulders sharp and architectural, the lapels re-cut into something aggressive and modern. It was punk meets couture, rebellion rendered in tailoring. And stitched into every visible seam, in thread that caught the workroom light, were letters.
H-E-L-P.

The code — the same code Bertha had hidden in the lining of Thornfield garments for years, her cry from the dark, her SOS stitched into silk where no one would see it. But here, in this jacket, it was different. The HELP wasn't hidden. It was decoration. It was pattern. It was a signature. Each seam declared it: the shoulder seam, the side seam, the cuff, the center back. The letters marched across the fabric like a refrain, and the refrain said: I made this. Mine.

Jane reached out and touched the sleeve. The stitching was immaculate — finer than anything that left the Thornfield atelier, because this was Bertha's own hand, her own time, her own intention poured into every centimeter.

"For the runway," Bertha said, and her voice had gone quiet and sure, the voice of a woman who had made a decision and was living inside it. "When the time comes. I want to walk out in something that tells the truth. Not the truth they wrote for me. The truth I made."
Jane looked at the jacket. She thought of the crimson dress hanging behind its wall of grey. Two garments. Two women. Two acts of preservation in a house that wanted them erased.
"It's extraordinary," she said.
Bertha zipped the bag and returned it to its hiding place under the table. "It's a start."

Later, alone in the suite, Jane lay in the darkness and listened to the château settle around her — the creak of timbers, the distant tick of pipes, the low hum of the Loire wind finding every crack in the ancient stone.

The bed was vast. Edward lay beside her, breathing evenly, one arm flung across the mattress between them like a low fence. He was asleep. He'd fallen asleep certain of the world he'd built: his atelier, his IPO, his Jane in her Thornfield colours, his aesthetic intact.
She turned her head toward the closet.

The dress was there. She couldn't see it — the room was dark, the closet door closed — but she could feel it the way you feel a heartbeat in a quiet room, the presence of something alive and waiting. Crimson. Raw silk. Violent and vital and absolutely hers.
And below, in the cool dark of the basement, the punk tuxedo was taking shape — needle-sharp and unflinching, its seams spelling out a word that had once been a prayer and was becoming a war cry.
Two dresses. Two women.

She touched the ring on her finger — cool gold, old weight, the promise Edward had made. Then she reached across the sheet and touched the closet door, her fingers finding the edge of the panel in the dark.
Two promises. Two weapons.

The red dress hidden in the closet, waiting. The punk tuxedo in the basement, ready. When the moment came — and it would come, she felt it building like a season — they would each wear the thing that made them most themselves. The thing he'd tried to iron out of them. The thing that didn't suit the aesthetic.
The thing that didn't fit the cage.
Jane closed her eyes, and for the first time since she'd arrived at Thornfield, the silence didn't feel like a weapon.
It felt like a countdown.
